readings.title: First reading
readings.text: While Apollos was in Corinth, Paul made his way overland as far as Ephesus, where he found a number of disciples. When he asked, ‘Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you became believers?’ they answered, ‘No, we were never even told there was such a thing as a Holy Spirit.’ ‘Then how were you baptised?’ he asked. ‘With John’s baptism’ they replied. ‘John’s baptism’ said Paul ‘was a baptism of repentance; but he insisted that the people should believe in the one who was to come after him – in other words, Jesus.’ When they heard this, they were baptised in the name of the Lord Jesus, and the moment Paul had laid hands on them the Holy Spirit came down on them, and they began to speak with tongues and to prophesy. There were about twelve of these men.

exegesis.summary: Paul arrives in Ephesus and discovers disciples who knew only the baptism of John. After explaining that John pointed forward to Christ, Paul baptizes them in the name of the Lord Jesus and lays hands on them, and the Holy Spirit descends with the gifts of tongues and prophecy. The episode shows that the baptism of repentance must be completed by the Sacrament instituted by Christ, which alone confers the Holy Spirit.

patristicQuotes.quote: The Apostle, having found certain Ephesians baptized with John's baptism, baptized them again in the name of Christ, because they had not been baptized in the Spirit. As the baptism of Christ was more than that of John, it was a new one given, not the same repeated.

So then we baptize again after John's baptism; after a homicide's we baptize not: because John gave his own baptism, the homicide gives Christ's; which is so holy a sacrament, that not even a homicide's ministration can pollute it.

cccReferences.title: Baptism unites us to Christ's death and resurrection
Laying on of hands and the gift of the Spirit
Baptism from the day of Pentecost

exegesis.thematicConnection: On the threshold of Pentecost, this reading shows what the Ascended Christ pours out on His Church: not merely a baptism of repentance but the Holy Spirit Himself, the Gift of the glorified Lord who now reigns at the Father's right hand.

readings.title: Responsorial Psalm
readings.text:
Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.
or
Alleluia!
Let God arise, let his foes be scattered.
Let those who hate him flee before him.
As smoke is blown away so will they be blown away;
like wax that melts before the fire,
so the wicked shall perish at the presence of God.
Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.
or
Alleluia!
But the just shall rejoice at the presence of God,
they shall exult and dance for joy.
O sing to the Lord, make music to his name;
rejoice in the Lord, exult at his presence.
Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.
or
Alleluia!
Father of the orphan, defender of the widow,
such is God in his holy place.
God gives the lonely a home to live in;
he leads the prisoners forth into freedom.
Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.
or
Alleluia!
readings.refrain: Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.

exegesis.summary: The psalm sings of God's triumphal procession: His enemies scatter like smoke before Him, while the just exult and dance for joy. He is the Father of orphans and defender of widows, leading prisoners into freedom. The Church hears this psalm as a prophecy of Christ's Ascension, when the King takes His throne and the kingdoms of the earth are summoned to praise.

patristicQuotes.quote: The kingdom of God may stand for Christ Himself, whom we day by day wish to come… As He is our resurrection, because in Him we rise again, so may He be called the kingdom of God, because we are to reign in Him.
The kingdom of God means that He Himself is placed in the midst of them, that is, reigning in their hearts by faith.

cccReferences.title: Christ reigns yet awaits final fulfillment
Kingdom, power, and glory restored to the Father

exegesis.thematicConnection: The psalm provides the soundtrack of the Ascension: 'Kingdoms of the earth, sing to God.' What the apostles witness in Acts and what Jesus promises in the Gospel — His victory and the gift of the Spirit — the psalm proclaims in song.

readings.title: Gospel
readings.text:
His disciples said to Jesus, ‘Now you are speaking plainly and not using metaphors! Now we see that you know everything, and do not have to wait for questions to be put into words; because of this we believe that you came from God.’ Jesus answered them:
‘Do you believe at last?
Listen; the time will come – in fact it has come already –
when you will be scattered,
each going his own way and leaving me alone.
And yet I am not alone,
because the Father is with me.
I have told you all this
so that you may find peace in me.
In the world you will have trouble,
but be brave: I have conquered the world.’

exegesis.summary: At the close of the Last Supper Discourse, the disciples claim a clarity of faith they do not yet possess. Jesus gently warns that they will scatter and leave Him alone, yet He is never alone because the Father is with Him. He concludes with the words that resound on this Solemnity: 'In the world you will have trouble, but be brave: I have conquered the world.'

patristicQuotes.quote: He left no peace in this world; in which we conquer the enemy, and have love one to another: He will give us peace in the world to come, when we shall reign without an enemy. This peace is Himself, both when we believe that He is, and when we shall see Him as He is.
He says this to console His disciples, who were now troubled at the prospect of the hatred and opposition which awaited them after His departure.

cccReferences.title: United to Christ's will, we share His victory
Christ's filial prayer in the hour of self-gift
The Kingdom defeats the powers of evil

exegesis.thematicConnection: On the Solemnity of the Ascension, Christ's farewell words become the charter of the Church He leaves behind. He goes to the Father not to abandon us but to send the Spirit (the First Reading) and to reign as the conquering King the psalm extols.

synthesis.title: The Conqueror Ascends and Pours Out His Spirit
synthesis.body:
Today the Church lifts her eyes to the Lord who is taken up in glory. The Ascension is not Christ's absence but His enthronement: 'Let God arise, let His foes be scattered.' The psalm's triumphal procession is fulfilled as the Risen Jesus, having conquered sin and death, ascends to the right hand of the Father and from there leads captives into freedom. 

In the Gospel, on the eve of His Passion, Jesus already speaks as the Victor: 'I have conquered the world.' He warns His disciples that they will scatter — and the Ascension itself could feel like such a scattering — yet He is never alone, for the Father is with Him, and through the Spirit He will be with them always. The peace He gives is His own peace, the peace of the One enthroned above every power. 

The reading from Acts shows that promise unfolding. The Ascended Lord does not leave His Church orphaned: through Paul's hands at Ephesus, the Holy Spirit descends upon those baptized into Jesus' name. What John's baptism could only foreshadow, the glorified Christ now accomplishes. So we who wait in these days before Pentecost take courage: our King reigns, His Spirit is poured forth, and in Him we find peace.
